# Mermaid Diagram Guidelines This document provides comprehensive guidelines for creating and validating Mermaid diagrams in markdown files. ## Validation Before committing, always validate your mermaid diagrams: ```bash mm --validate . ``` ## Critical Rules ### 1. Forward Slashes in Labels **NEVER use forward slashes (`/`) in node labels** - they cause lexical errors in Mermaid parsers. ```mermaid # ✅ CORRECT graph LR A["Pub Sub"] B["TCP Syslog on port 514"] C["var run vector.sock"] D["Redis and MySQL"] E["New or Return"] F["https:example.com"] # ❌ WRONG - causes parsing errors graph LR A["Pub/Sub"] B["TCP Syslog/Port 514"] C["/var/run/vector.sock"] D["Redis/MySQL"] E["New/Return"] F["https://example.com"] ``` **Solutions for common cases:** - File paths: Replace `/` with spaces: `/var/run/file` → `var run file` - Alternatives: Replace `/` with spaces: `Pub/Sub` → `Pub Sub` - Or/And separators: Use words: `New/Return` → `New or Return` - URLs: Remove `//`: `https://example.com` → `https:example.com` ### 2. Multi-line Labels **Always use actual newlines for multi-line labels** - NOT `
` or `#10;` ```mermaid # ✅ CORRECT - use actual newlines within quotes graph LR A["First line Second line Third line"] B["Database Storage Layer Persistent"] C["IOLSession Master Session"] D["Thread-Safe Queue Event Buffer"] # ❌ WRONG - causes parsing errors graph LR A["First line
Second line
Third line"] B["Database#10;Storage Layer#10;Persistent"] C[IOLSession
Master Session] # Missing quotes +
D["Thread-Safe Queue
Event Buffer"] # Has quotes but
``` **Important:** - The `
` tag contains a forward slash which triggers parsing errors! - **NEVER use `
` in graph/flowchart node labels** - always use actual newlines - Multi-line labels MUST be quoted: use `["text\nmore"]` not `[text\nmore]` - This is different from sequence diagrams where `
` IS allowed in Notes only ### 3. Quote Complex Labels **Always quote labels that contain:** - Multiple lines - Special characters - Spaces at start/end - Punctuation ```mermaid # ✅ CORRECT graph LR A["Complex Label With Multiple Lines"] B["Label with special chars: &*%"] C[" Padded Label "] # ❌ WRONG - unquoted multi-line graph LR A[Complex Label With Multiple Lines] ``` ### 4. Square Brackets in Labels **Use HTML entities to display square brackets correctly** - raw `[` and `]` cause parsing errors in Mermaid. ```mermaid # ✅ CORRECT - using HTML entities graph TD A -->|"array[0] == value"| B C["Check if byid[2] != 'P'"] # ❌ WRONG - raw square brackets graph TD A -->|"array[0] == value"| B C["Check if byid[2] != 'P'"] ``` **HTML entities for square brackets:** - `[` renders as `[` - `]` renders as `]` **Common use cases:** - Array indexing: `array[0]` → `array[0]` - String indexing: `byid[2]` → `byid[2]` - Bracket notation: `obj[key]` → `obj[key]` ## Diagram-Specific Rules ### Graph Diagrams (`graph TB`, `graph LR`) **CRITICAL: `
` is FORBIDDEN in graph node labels!** - ✅ Multi-line labels work with actual newlines in quotes - ❌ **NEVER use `
` in node labels** - use actual newlines instead - ✅ Escape or avoid special characters: `/`, `<`, `>`, `&` - ✅ Use descriptive connection labels: `A -->|Label| B` - ✅ Quote all multi-line labels: `["text\nmore"]` ```mermaid # ✅ CORRECT - actual newlines in quoted labels graph TB A["Node One Line two Line three"] B["IOLSession Master Session"] C["Thread-Safe Queue Event Buffer"] A -->|Connection Label| B B -->|Another Label| C # ❌ WRONG - using
causes parse errors graph TB A["Node One
Line two
Line three"] B[IOLSession
Master Session] ``` ### Sequence Diagrams (`sequenceDiagram`) **Sequence diagrams have STRICT parsing rules - avoid special characters in arrow messages!** #### Message Text Rules - ❌ **CANNOT use multi-line arrow messages** - must be single line - ❌ **AVOID semicolons (`;`) in arrow messages** - causes parse errors - ❌ **AVOID colons (`:`) in arrow messages** - use simple text only - ❌ **AVOID equals signs (`=`) in arrow messages** - causes parse errors - ❌ **AVOID angle brackets (`<`, `>`) in arrow messages** - causes parse errors - ❌ **NEVER use `
` in arrow messages** - the forward slash causes errors - ✅ **DO use `
` in Notes** - this DOES work for multi-line Notes - ✅ Keep all arrow message text simple and on the same line - ✅ Use parentheses for additional info in arrows instead of special chars - ✅ Use simplified text without punctuation in arrow messages - ✅ Put complex details in Notes instead of arrows ```mermaid # ✅ CORRECT - simple message text sequenceDiagram A->>B: Send data B->>C: Process request C-->>B: Return response Note over C: Processing complete # ✅ CORRECT - using parentheses for details sequenceDiagram A->>B: Message with details (additional info) B->>C: Another message (with context) # ❌ WRONG - special characters in arrow message sequenceDiagram A->>B: Set-Cookie: i00=value; SameSite=None B->>C: ID;LastAccess;TTL # ❌ WRONG - multi-line arrow message breaks parsing sequenceDiagram A->>B: Message with details Additional info on second line # ❌ WRONG -
in arrow message (forward slash!) sequenceDiagram A->>B: Send data
More details ``` #### Note Rules - ✅ **Use `
` in Notes for line breaks** - this DOES work in Notes - ❌ **Combine consecutive Notes** - multiple Notes before an action can cause issues - ✅ Keep Notes concise and before/after actions - ✅ Notes spanning participants: `Note over A,B: Message` ```mermaid # ✅ CORRECT - single Note with line breaks sequenceDiagram A->>B: Send request Note over B: Processing data
Computing result
Validating output B->>A: Return result # ✅ CORRECT - Notes at different points sequenceDiagram Note over A: Starting process A->>B: Send data Note over B: Data received B->>C: Forward data # ❌ WRONG - consecutive Notes before action sequenceDiagram A->>B: Send request Note over B: First note Note over B: Second note B->>A: Response # ✅ FIXED - combine into single Note sequenceDiagram A->>B: Send request Note over B: First note
Second note B->>A: Response ``` #### Arrow Message Best Practices **Keep arrow messages simple and descriptive:** ```mermaid # ✅ CORRECT - simple, clear messages sequenceDiagram Browser->>Server: HTTP Request Server->>Database: Query data Database-->>Server: Return results Server-->>Browser: HTTP Response # ❌ WRONG - too much detail in arrow sequenceDiagram Browser->>Server: HTTP GET /api/users?id=123&format=json Server->>Database: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=123 Database-->>Server: {id:123, name:"John", email:"john@example.com"} Server-->>Browser: 200 OK {"status":"success","data":{...}} # ✅ FIXED - details in Notes instead sequenceDiagram Browser->>Server: HTTP Request Note over Browser,Server: GET /api/users?id=123 Server->>Database: Query users table Note over Server,Database: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=123 Database-->>Server: Return user data Server-->>Browser: JSON Response Note over Server,Browser: Status 200 OK with user object ``` ### State Diagrams (`stateDiagram-v2`) - ✅ Multi-line transition labels work with actual newlines - ✅ Avoid slashes in state names - ✅ Quote complex labels ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> StateA StateA --> StateB: Transition label Line two of label StateB --> [*]: Done ``` ## Common Mistakes to Avoid 1. **Using `
` in graph/flowchart node labels**: ⚠️ **MOST COMMON ERROR** - use actual newlines instead 2. **Using HTML tags in graph nodes**: `
`, ``, `` cause slash parsing issues 3. **Using `#10;` for newlines**: Doesn't work in all renderers 4. **Unquoted multi-line labels in graphs**: Must quote: `["line1\nline2"]` not `[line1\nline2]` 5. **Multi-line arrow messages in sequence diagrams**: Not supported, keep on one line 6. **Special characters in sequence diagram arrows**: `;`, `:`, `=`, `<`, `>`, `()` cause parse errors 7. **Consecutive Notes in sequence diagrams**: Combine into single Note with `
` 8. **Using `
` in sequence diagram arrow messages**: Forward slash causes errors (Notes are OK) 9. **Unquoted paths**: Always quote paths and remove slashes: `/var/log/file` → `"var log file"` 10. **Slashes in labels**: Replace `/` with spaces, hyphens, or words like "or"/"and" 11. **URLs with slashes**: Remove `//` from URLs: `https://example.com` → `https:example.com` 12. **Square brackets in labels**: Use HTML entities `[` and `]` instead of raw `[` and `]` 13. **Parentheses in arrow labels**: Can cause issues - use simple text: `setMultiIdentifier()` → `Call setMultiIdentifier` ## Validation Checklist Before committing mermaid diagrams: **General:** - [ ] ⚠️ **NO `
` tags in graph/flowchart node labels** - use actual newlines - [ ] All multi-line labels use actual newlines (not `
` or `#10;`) - [ ] All multi-line labels are quoted: `["line1\nline2"]` - [ ] No forward slashes in node labels (use spaces or alternative punctuation) - [ ] Square brackets use HTML entities (`[` and `]`) - [ ] Complex labels are quoted with `["label"]` - [ ] Diagram renders correctly in browser/preview - [ ] Run validation tool: `mm --validate .` (if available) **Sequence Diagrams Specific:** - [ ] Arrow messages are single-line only (no multi-line) - [ ] No special characters in arrow messages (`;`, `:`, `=`, `<`, `>`) - [ ] No `
` tags in arrow messages (use in Notes only) - [ ] Consecutive Notes combined into single Note with `
` - [ ] Complex details moved to Notes instead of arrow messages - [ ] Participant names are simple (no special characters) - [ ] `
` used in Notes for multi-line content (this IS allowed) ## Complete Examples ### ✅ Good Example ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Input Processing" A["Syslog Input Port 514 TCP"] B["Add Message ID UUIDv7 generation"] end subgraph "Storage" C["Redis In-memory cache"] D["MySQL Persistent storage"] end subgraph "Output" E["GCP Pub Sub Topic: messages"] end A --> B B --> C B --> D C --> E D --> E ``` ### ❌ Bad Example (Multiple Issues) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Input Processing" A["Syslog Input
Port 514/TCP"] B[Add Message ID#10;UUIDv7 generation] end subgraph "Storage" C["Redis/MySQL
Dual storage"] end subgraph "Output" E["GCP Pub/Sub
Topic: messages"] end A --> B B --> C C --> E ``` **Issues in bad example:** 1. Uses `
` instead of actual newlines (line A) 2. Uses `#10;` which doesn't work in all renderers (line B) 3. Has forward slash in "514/TCP" (line A) 4. Has forward slash in "Pub/Sub" (line E) 5. Has forward slash in "Redis/MySQL" (line C) 6. Unquoted label with special characters (line B) **Fixed version:** ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Input Processing" A["Syslog Input Port 514 TCP"] B["Add Message ID UUIDv7 generation"] end subgraph "Storage" C["Redis and MySQL Dual storage"] end subgraph "Output" E["GCP Pub Sub Topic: messages"] end A --> B B --> C C --> E ``` ## Real-World Example: Sequence Diagram Fix **Before (Broken - Parse Errors):** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram A->>B: setI00([], yc) B->>C: get(13) C-->>B: 13 hex random chars Note over B: Generate Cookie ID:
CubeNr(4) + RandId(13) + Time + Serial(4) Note over B: Create cookie value
ID;LastAccess;TTL B->>A: Set-Cookie: i00=; SameSite=None; Secure; path=/; domain=.ioam.de ``` **Issues:** 1. **Arrow message has special characters:** `:`, `=`, `;`, `<`, `>`, `/` → Parser fails 2. **Two consecutive Notes** before next action → Can cause parsing issues 3. **Arrow message too long and complex** → Hard to read **After (Fixed - Works):** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram A->>B: setI00([], yc) B->>C: get(13) C-->>B: 13 hex random chars Note over B: Generate Cookie ID:
CubeNr(4) + RandId(13) + Time + Serial(4)
Format: ID-LastAccess-TTL B->>A: Set-Cookie with options Note over A,B: Options: SameSite=None, Secure,
path=/, domain=.example.com ``` **Fixes:** 1. **Simplified arrow message:** "Set-Cookie with options" (no special chars) 2. **Combined two Notes into one** with `
` 3. **Moved technical details to separate Note** after the action 4. **Result:** Clean, readable, parseable diagram ## Additional Resources - [Mermaid Official Documentation](https://mermaid.js.org/) - [Mermaid Live Editor](https://mermaid.live/) - Test your diagrams before committing - Project validation tool: `mm --validate ` ## Summary of Key Changes When fixing existing diagrams: **Graph/Flowchart Diagrams:** 1. **Remove all `
` tags from node labels** → Use actual newlines within quotes 2. **Remove all `#10;` sequences** → Use actual newlines within quotes 3. **Remove all forward slashes** → Use spaces, hyphens, or words like "or"/"and" 4. **Replace square brackets with HTML entities** → `[` becomes `[` and `]` becomes `]` 5. **Quote all multi-line labels** → Use `["label"]` syntax 6. **Remove slashes from URLs** → `https://site.com` → `https:site.com` 7. **Remove leading slashes from paths** → `/var/run/file` → `var run file` **Sequence Diagrams:** 1. **Keep arrow messages simple** → No special characters (`;`, `:`, `=`, `<`, `>`) 2. **Keep arrow messages single-line** → Use parentheses for additional context 3. **Move complex details to Notes** → Use `Note over Participant: details` 4. **Use `
` in Notes only** → Notes CAN have `
` but arrows cannot 5. **Combine consecutive Notes** → Two `Note over X` → One `Note over X: line1
line2` 6. **Simplify arrow text** → "Set-Cookie: i00=value" → "Set-Cookie header" 7. **Use Notes for technical details** → Keep arrows readable, Notes detailed

1→# Sandcastle Networking
     2→
     3→Sandcastle runs an **isolated Docker daemon** ("dockyard") separate from the host's system Docker. All container networking — sandboxes, terminals, VNC, Tailscale — runs through this daemon and is confined to RFC 1918 private IP ranges that you control.
     4→
     5→## Configuration variables
     6→
     7→Set these in `sandcastle.env` before running `installer.sh install`.
     8→
     9→### `SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET` (default: `10.89.0.0/16`)
    10→
    11→The **single knob** for all Sandcastle networking. Every Docker network is carved from this /16. Change it if the default conflicts with your LAN or existing Docker setup.
    12→
    13→Must be an RFC 1918 private range:
    14→
    15→| Range | Example value |
    16→|---|---|
    17→| Class A | `10.89.0.0/16` (default) |
    18→| Class B | `172.20.0.0/16` |
    19→| Class C | `192.168.89.0/16`* |
    20→
    21→> \* 192.168.x.x is a /16 in the RFC but many networks are already in use there — prefer 10.x.x.x or 172.16–31.x.x.
    22→
    23→The installer automatically derives `DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR`, `DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR`, and `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` from this value. You only need the individual overrides below if you need fine-grained control.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→### Derived variables (override only if needed)
    28→
    29→#### `DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR` (derived: `<net>.0.1/24`)
    30→
    31→The **gateway IP and mask** for dockyard's default bridge interface (`sc_docker0` on the host). This is what Docker calls `bip`. Every container on the default bridge reaches the host via this IP.
    32→
    33→Example for `10.89.0.0/16`: `10.89.0.1/24`
    34→
    35→#### `DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR` (derived: `<net>.0.0/24`)
    36→
    37→The **container address range** on the default bridge. Limits IPs Docker assigns to containers here, keeping them predictable and distinct from subnets allocated to user-defined networks.
    38→
    39→Example for `10.89.0.0/16`: `10.89.0.0/24`
    40→
    41→#### `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` (derived: same as `SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET`)
    42→
    43→The **address pool** from which dockyard allocates subnets for every user-defined Docker network (`sandcastle-web`, `sc-ts-net-*`, etc.). When a network is created without an explicit subnet, Docker carves a free `/24` from this range.
    44→
    45→#### `DOCKYARD_POOL_SIZE` (default: `24`)
    46→
    47→The prefix length of each subnet allocated from the pool. `/24` gives 254 usable container IPs per network and allows up to 256 networks within a `/16` pool.
    48→
    49→---
    50→
    51→## Runtime networks
    52→
    53→These Docker networks are created automatically — no configuration needed.
    54→
    55→### `sandcastle-web`
    56→
    57→Created once by the installer. Subnet auto-allocated from `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` (first available /24).
    58→
    59→The internal "service bus" — anything that needs to talk to anything else goes through here:
    60→
    61→| Container | Purpose |
    62→|---|---|
    63→| `sandcastle-web` | Rails app |
    64→| `sc-wetty-{user}-{sandbox}` | Web terminal sidecars (SSH into sandbox) |
    65→| `sc-novnc-{sandbox}` | noVNC browser UI containers |
    66→| sandbox containers | Joined on demand when a terminal or VNC session opens |
    67→
    68→Traefik routes external HTTPS traffic into this network.
    69→
    70→### `sc-ts-net-{username}`
    71→
    72→Created by `TailscaleManager` when a user enables Tailscale. One network per user, each with an **explicitly assigned /24** derived from `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE`:
    73→
    74→```
    75→base = DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE   # e.g. 10.89.0.0/16
    76→subnet = oct1.oct2.(1 + user_id % 255).0/24
    77→
    78→# user_id=1  → 10.89.2.0/24
    79→# user_id=2  → 10.89.3.0/24
    80→```
    81→
    82→| Container | Purpose |
    83→|---|---|
    84→| `sc-ts-{username}` | Tailscale sidecar — joins user's tailnet, advertises the /24 as a subnet route |
    85→| sandbox containers | Joined when `tailscale_auto_connect` is enabled |
    86→
    87→Because these subnets sit within `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE`, dockyard's iptables NAT rules cover them automatically — sandbox containers get outbound internet.
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## Network layout (default)
    92→
    93→```
    94→SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET: 10.89.0.0/16
    95→│
    96→├── sc_docker0  (dockyard default bridge)
    97→│   ├── gateway (DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR):    10.89.0.1/24
    98→│   └── containers (DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR):  10.89.0.x
    99→│
   100→├── sandcastle-web  (pool-allocated, e.g. 10.89.1.0/24)
   101→│   ├── sandcastle-web container  (Rails app)
   102→│   ├── sc-wetty-*                (terminal sidecars)
   103→│   ├── sc-novnc-*                (VNC sidecars)
   104→│   └── sandbox containers        (joined on demand)
   105→│
   106→└── sc-ts-net-{user}  (explicit /24 per user)
   107→    ├── e.g. user_id=1 → 10.89.2.0/24
   108→    ├── sc-ts-{username}      (Tailscale sidecar)
   109→    └── sandbox containers    (if tailscale_auto_connect)
   110→```
   111→
   112→---
   113→
   114→## How to configure during install
   115→
   116→### Quickstart (change the private range)
   117→
   118→```bash
   119→# 1. Generate config
   120→sudo installer.sh gen-env
   121→
   122→# 2. Change the private network
   123→sed -i 's|SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=.*|SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=172.20.0.0/16|' sandcastle.env
   124→
   125→# 3. Install
   126→sudo installer.sh install
   127→```
   128→
   129→Or edit `sandcastle.env` manually — the relevant section looks like:
   130→
   131→```bash
   132→# ─── Dockyard (Docker + Sysbox) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
   133→
   134→DOCKYARD_ROOT=/sandcastle
   135→DOCKYARD_DOCKER_PREFIX=sc_
   136→
   137→# Private /16 from which all Sandcastle Docker networks are carved.
   138→# Must be RFC 1918: 10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x, or 192.168.x.x.
   139→SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=10.89.0.0/16
   140→#DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR=10.89.0.1/24   # uncomment to override
   141→#DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR=10.89.0.0/24    # uncomment to override
   142→#DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE=10.89.0.0/16     # uncomment to override
   143→DOCKYARD_POOL_SIZE=24
   144→```
   145→
   146→### Fine-grained override example
   147→
   148→If you want the bridge on one range and the pool on another (e.g. bridge on `10.42.x.x`, pool on `10.89.x.x`):
   149→
   150→```bash
   151→SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=10.89.0.0/16    # pool stays here
   152→DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR=10.42.0.1/24     # override bridge to a different range
   153→DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR=10.42.0.0/24
   154→```
   155→
   156→### Changing the network on an existing install
   157→
   158→> **Warning:** changing these values after install requires destroying and recreating dockyard (the isolated Docker daemon) because Docker bakes the address pool into its state. This will also recreate the `sandcastle-web` network, briefly interrupting running sandboxes.
   159→
   160→```bash
   161→# Update sandcastle.env with the new SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET value, then:
   162→sudo installer.sh reset
   163→```
   164→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

# Sandcastle Networking

Sandcastle runs an **isolated Docker daemon** ("dockyard") separate from the host's system Docker. All container networking — sandboxes, terminals, VNC, Tailscale — runs through this daemon and is confined to RFC 1918 private IP ranges that you control.

## Configuration variables

Set these in `sandcastle.env` before running `installer.sh install`.

### `SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET` (default: `10.89.0.0/16`)

The **single knob** for all Sandcastle networking. Every Docker network is carved from this /16. Change it if the default conflicts with your LAN or existing Docker setup.

Must be an RFC 1918 private range:

| Range | Example value |
|---|---|
| Class A | `10.89.0.0/16` (default) |
| Class B | `172.20.0.0/16` |
| Class C | `192.168.89.0/16`* |

> \* 192.168.x.x is a /16 in the RFC but many networks are already in use there — prefer 10.x.x.x or 172.16–31.x.x.

The installer automatically derives `DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR`, `DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR`, and `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` from this value. You only need the individual overrides below if you need fine-grained control.

---

### Derived variables (override only if needed)

#### `DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR` (derived: `<net>.0.1/24`)

The **gateway IP and mask** for dockyard's default bridge interface (`sc_docker0` on the host). This is what Docker calls `bip`. Every container on the default bridge reaches the host via this IP.

Example for `10.89.0.0/16`: `10.89.0.1/24`

#### `DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR` (derived: `<net>.0.0/24`)

The **container address range** on the default bridge. Limits IPs Docker assigns to containers here, keeping them predictable and distinct from subnets allocated to user-defined networks.

Example for `10.89.0.0/16`: `10.89.0.0/24`

#### `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` (derived: same as `SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET`)

The **address pool** from which dockyard allocates subnets for every user-defined Docker network (`sandcastle-web`, `sc-ts-net-*`, etc.). When a network is created without an explicit subnet, Docker carves a free `/24` from this range.

#### `DOCKYARD_POOL_SIZE` (default: `24`)

The prefix length of each subnet allocated from the pool. `/24` gives 254 usable container IPs per network and allows up to 256 networks within a `/16` pool.

---

## Runtime networks

These Docker networks are created automatically — no configuration needed.

### `sandcastle-web`

Created once by the installer. Subnet auto-allocated from `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` (first available /24).

The internal "service bus" — anything that needs to talk to anything else goes through here:

| Container | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sandcastle-web` | Rails app |
| `sc-wetty-{user}-{sandbox}` | Web terminal sidecars (SSH into sandbox) |
| `sc-novnc-{sandbox}` | noVNC browser UI containers |
| sandbox containers | Joined on demand when a terminal or VNC session opens |

Traefik routes external HTTPS traffic into this network.

### `sc-ts-net-{username}`

Created by `TailscaleManager` when a user enables Tailscale. One network per user, each with an **explicitly assigned /24** derived from `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE`:

```
base = DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE   # e.g. 10.89.0.0/16
subnet = oct1.oct2.(1 + user_id % 255).0/24

# user_id=1  → 10.89.2.0/24
# user_id=2  → 10.89.3.0/24
```

| Container | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sc-ts-{username}` | Tailscale sidecar — joins user's tailnet, advertises the /24 as a subnet route |
| sandbox containers | Joined when `tailscale_auto_connect` is enabled |

Because these subnets sit within `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE`, dockyard's iptables NAT rules cover them automatically — sandbox containers get outbound internet.

---

## Network layout (default)

```
SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET: 10.89.0.0/16
│
├── sc_docker0  (dockyard default bridge)
│   ├── gateway (DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR):    10.89.0.1/24
│   └── containers (DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR):  10.89.0.x
│
├── sandcastle-web  (pool-allocated, e.g. 10.89.1.0/24)
│   ├── sandcastle-web container  (Rails app)
│   ├── sc-wetty-*                (terminal sidecars)
│   ├── sc-novnc-*                (VNC sidecars)
│   └── sandbox containers        (joined on demand)
│
└── sc-ts-net-{user}  (explicit /24 per user)
    ├── e.g. user_id=1 → 10.89.2.0/24
    ├── sc-ts-{username}      (Tailscale sidecar)
    └── sandbox containers    (if tailscale_auto_connect)
```

---

## How to configure during install

### Quickstart (change the private range)

```bash
# 1. Generate config
sudo installer.sh gen-env

# 2. Change the private network
sed -i 's|SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=.*|SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=172.20.0.0/16|' sandcastle.env

# 3. Install
sudo installer.sh install
```

Or edit `sandcastle.env` manually — the relevant section looks like:

```bash
# ─── Dockyard (Docker + Sysbox) ──────────────────────────────────────────────

DOCKYARD_ROOT=/sandcastle
DOCKYARD_DOCKER_PREFIX=sc_

# Private /16 from which all Sandcastle Docker networks are carved.
# Must be RFC 1918: 10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x, or 192.168.x.x.
SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=10.89.0.0/16
#DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR=10.89.0.1/24   # uncomment to override
#DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR=10.89.0.0/24    # uncomment to override
#DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE=10.89.0.0/16     # uncomment to override
DOCKYARD_POOL_SIZE=24
```

### Fine-grained override example

If you want the bridge on one range and the pool on another (e.g. bridge on `10.42.x.x`, pool on `10.89.x.x`):

```bash
SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=10.89.0.0/16    # pool stays here
DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR=10.42.0.1/24     # override bridge to a different range
DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR=10.42.0.0/24
```

### Changing the network on an existing install

> **Warning:** changing these values after install requires destroying and recreating dockyard (the isolated Docker daemon) because Docker bakes the address pool into its state. This will also recreate the `sandcastle-web` network, briefly interrupting running sandboxes.

```bash
# Update sandcastle.env with the new SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET value, then:
sudo installer.sh reset
```

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Sandcastle Networking

Sandcastle runs an **isolated Docker daemon** ("dockyard") separate from the host's system Docker. All container networking — sandboxes, terminals, VNC, Tailscale — runs through this daemon and is confined to RFC 1918 private IP ranges that you control.

## Configuration variables

Set these in `sandcastle.env` before running `installer.sh install`.

### `SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET` (default: `10.89.0.0/16`)

The **single knob** for all Sandcastle networking. Every Docker network is carved from this /16. Change it if the default conflicts with your LAN or existing Docker setup.

Must be an RFC 1918 private range:

| Range | Example value |
|---|---|
| Class A | `10.89.0.0/16` (default) |
| Class B | `172.20.0.0/16` |
| Class C | `192.168.89.0/16`* |

> \* 192.168.x.x is a /16 in the RFC but many networks are already in use there — prefer 10.x.x.x or 172.16–31.x.x.

The installer automatically derives `DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR`, `DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR`, and `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` from this value. You only need the individual overrides below if you need fine-grained control.

---

### Derived variables (override only if needed)

#### `DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR` (derived: `<net>.0.1/24`)

The **gateway IP and mask** for dockyard's default bridge interface (`sc_docker0` on the host). This is what Docker calls `bip`. Every container on the default bridge reaches the host via this IP.

Example for `10.89.0.0/16`: `10.89.0.1/24`

#### `DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR` (derived: `<net>.0.0/24`)

The **container address range** on the default bridge. Limits IPs Docker assigns to containers here, keeping them predictable and distinct from subnets allocated to user-defined networks.

Example for `10.89.0.0/16`: `10.89.0.0/24`

#### `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` (derived: same as `SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET`)

The **address pool** from which dockyard allocates subnets for every user-defined Docker network (`sandcastle-web`, `sc-ts-net-*`, etc.). When a network is created without an explicit subnet, Docker carves a free `/24` from this range.

#### `DOCKYARD_POOL_SIZE` (default: `24`)

The prefix length of each subnet allocated from the pool. `/24` gives 254 usable container IPs per network and allows up to 256 networks within a `/16` pool.

---

## Runtime networks

These Docker networks are created automatically — no configuration needed.

### `sandcastle-web`

Created once by the installer. Subnet auto-allocated from `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE` (first available /24).

The internal "service bus" — anything that needs to talk to anything else goes through here:

| Container | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sandcastle-web` | Rails app |
| `sc-wetty-{user}-{sandbox}` | Web terminal sidecars (SSH into sandbox) |
| `sc-novnc-{sandbox}` | noVNC browser UI containers |
| sandbox containers | Joined on demand when a terminal or VNC session opens |

Traefik routes external HTTPS traffic into this network.

### `sc-ts-net-{username}`

Created by `TailscaleManager` when a user enables Tailscale. One network per user, each with an **explicitly assigned /24** derived from `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE`:

```
base = DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE   # e.g. 10.89.0.0/16
subnet = oct1.oct2.(1 + user_id % 255).0/24

# user_id=1  → 10.89.2.0/24
# user_id=2  → 10.89.3.0/24
```

| Container | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sc-ts-{username}` | Tailscale sidecar — joins user's tailnet, advertises the /24 as a subnet route |
| sandbox containers | Joined when `tailscale_auto_connect` is enabled |

Because these subnets sit within `DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE`, dockyard's iptables NAT rules cover them automatically — sandbox containers get outbound internet.

---

## Network layout (default)

```
SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET: 10.89.0.0/16
│
├── sc_docker0  (dockyard default bridge)
│   ├── gateway (DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR):    10.89.0.1/24
│   └── containers (DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR):  10.89.0.x
│
├── sandcastle-web  (pool-allocated, e.g. 10.89.1.0/24)
│   ├── sandcastle-web container  (Rails app)
│   ├── sc-wetty-*                (terminal sidecars)
│   ├── sc-novnc-*                (VNC sidecars)
│   └── sandbox containers        (joined on demand)
│
└── sc-ts-net-{user}  (explicit /24 per user)
    ├── e.g. user_id=1 → 10.89.2.0/24
    ├── sc-ts-{username}      (Tailscale sidecar)
    └── sandbox containers    (if tailscale_auto_connect)
```

---

## How to configure during install

### Quickstart (change the private range)

```bash
# 1. Generate config
sudo installer.sh gen-env

# 2. Change the private network
sed -i 's|SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=.*|SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=172.20.0.0/16|' sandcastle.env

# 3. Install
sudo installer.sh install
```

Or edit `sandcastle.env` manually — the relevant section looks like:

```bash
# ─── Dockyard (Docker + Sysbox) ──────────────────────────────────────────────

DOCKYARD_ROOT=/sandcastle
DOCKYARD_DOCKER_PREFIX=sc_

# Private /16 from which all Sandcastle Docker networks are carved.
# Must be RFC 1918: 10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x, or 192.168.x.x.
SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=10.89.0.0/16
#DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR=10.89.0.1/24   # uncomment to override
#DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR=10.89.0.0/24    # uncomment to override
#DOCKYARD_POOL_BASE=10.89.0.0/16     # uncomment to override
DOCKYARD_POOL_SIZE=24
```

### Fine-grained override example

If you want the bridge on one range and the pool on another (e.g. bridge on `10.42.x.x`, pool on `10.89.x.x`):

```bash
SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET=10.89.0.0/16    # pool stays here
DOCKYARD_BRIDGE_CIDR=10.42.0.1/24     # override bridge to a different range
DOCKYARD_FIXED_CIDR=10.42.0.0/24
```

### Changing the network on an existing install

> **Warning:** changing these values after install requires destroying and recreating dockyard (the isolated Docker daemon) because Docker bakes the address pool into its state. This will also recreate the `sandcastle-web` network, briefly interrupting running sandboxes.

```bash
# Update sandcastle.env with the new SANDCASTLE_PRIVATE_NET value, then:
sudo installer.sh reset
```

EOF

end
    if sandbox.persistent_volume && sandbox.volume_path
      FileUtils.mkdir_p(sandbox.volume_path)
      FileUtils.chmod(0o777, sandbox.volume_path)
    end
    # Chrome profile persistence: mount .config/google-chrome separately if not mounting full home
    if user.chrome_persist_profile? && !sandbox.mount_home
      dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{user.name}/chrome-profile"
      FileUtils.mkdir_p(dir)
      FileUtils.chmod(0o777, dir)
    end
  rescue Errno::EACCES, Errno::ENOENT => e
    raise Error, "Failed to create mount directories: #{e.message}"
  end

  def destroy(sandbox:, keep_volume: false)
    begin
      TerminalManager.new.close(sandbox: sandbox)
    rescue TerminalManager::Error, Docker::Error::DockerError
      # best-effort terminal cleanup
    end

    begin
      VncManager.new.close(sandbox: sandbox)
    rescue VncManager::Error, Docker::Error::DockerError
      # best-effort VNC cleanup
    end

    RouteManager.new.remove_all_routes(sandbox: sandbox) if sandbox.routed?

    if sandbox.tailscale?
      TailscaleManager.new.disconnect_sandbox(sandbox: sandbox)
    end

    if sandbox.container_id.present?
      begin
        container = Docker::Container.get(sandbox.container_id)
        container.stop(t: 5) rescue nil
        container.delete(force: true)
      rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError

file.content:
class BtrfsHelper
  class Error < StandardError; end

  DATA_DIR = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR", "/data")

  class << self
    # Check if a path is on a BTRFS filesystem
    def btrfs?(path = DATA_DIR)
      return @is_btrfs if defined?(@is_btrfs)

      result = system("stat -f -c %T #{path} 2>/dev/null | grep -q '^btrfs$'")
      @is_btrfs = result == true
    rescue StandardError => e
      Rails.logger.warn("BTRFS detection failed: #{e.message}")
      @is_btrfs = false
    end

    # Create a BTRFS subvolume for a user's directory
    # This creates the parent user directory as a subvolume
    def create_user_subvolume(username)
      return false unless btrfs?

      user_dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{username}"

      # Check if already a subvolume
      if subvolume?(user_dir)
        Rails.logger.debug("User directory is already a BTRFS subvolume: #{user_dir}")
        ensure_owned(user_dir)
        return true
      end

      if Dir.exist?(user_dir) && !Dir.empty?(user_dir)
        # Directory already has content — skip conversion to avoid root-ownership issues
        Rails.logger.info("Skipping BTRFS subvolume conversion for existing directory: #{user_dir}")
        return false
      end

      create_subvolume(user_dir)
    end

    # Create a BTRFS subvolume for a user's data subdirectory
    def create_user_data_subvolume(username, data_path)
      return false unless btrfs?

      # Ensure parent user directory exists as subvolume
      create_user_subvolume(username)

      data_dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{username}/data/#{data_path}".chomp("/")

      # Check if already a subvolume
      if subvolume?(data_dir)
        Rails.logger.debug("Data directory is already a BTRFS subvolume: #{data_dir}")
        ensure_owned(data_dir)
        return true
      end

      if Dir.exist?(data_dir) && !Dir.empty?(data_dir)
        # Directory already has content — skip conversion to avoid root-ownership issues
        Rails.logger.info("Skipping BTRFS subvolume conversion for existing directory: #{data_dir}")
        return false
      end

      create_subvolume(data_dir)
    end

    private

    # Check if a path is a BTRFS subvolume
    def subvolume?(path)
      return false unless Dir.exist?(path)

      result = system("/usr/bin/sudo /usr/bin/btrfs subvolume show #{path} >/dev/null 2>&1")
      result == true
    rescue StandardError => e
      Rails.logger.warn("BTRFS subvolume check failed for #{path}: #{e.message}")
      false
    end

    # Create a new BTRFS subvolume
    def create_subvolume(path)
      # Ensure parent directory exists
      parent = File.dirname(path)
      FileUtils.mkdir_p(parent) unless Dir.exist?(parent)

      # Create subvolume using sudo with full path
      output, status = run_sudo_command("/usr/bin/btrfs subvolume create #{path}")

      unless status.success?
        raise Error, "Failed to create BTRFS subvolume #{path}: #{output}"
      end

      ensure_owned(path)

      Rails.logger.info("Created BTRFS subvolume: #{path}")
      true
    rescue StandardError => e
      Rails.logger.error("Failed to create BTRFS subvolume #{path}: #{e.message}")
      # Fall back to regular directory
      FileUtils.mkdir_p(path) unless Dir.exist?(path)
      false
    end

    # Ensure the path is owned by the current process user
    def ensure_owned(path)
      return if File.stat(path).uid == Process.uid

      run_sudo_command("/usr/bin/chown #{Process.uid}:#{Process.gid} #{path}")
    end

    # Run a command with sudo
    def run_sudo_command(command)
      full_command = "/usr/bin/sudo -n #{command}"
      output = `#{full_command} 2>&1`
      [ output, $? ]
    rescue StandardError => e
      [ e.message, nil ]
    end
  end
end

My fix is correct. Let me also check: is this actually a BTRFS filesystem? The log says "Skipping BTRFS subvolume conversion" which means BTRFS detection returned true. So it IS a BTRFS filesystem.

Now let me implement the fix:
1. Fix `btrfs_helper.rb` to call `ensure_owned` in the "skip conversion" case
2. I need to update the live container to apply this fix and verify it resolves the permission issue.

content.text:
Clear picture. `/sandcastle/data/users/thies/` is `root:root` `755`. The Rails container runs as uid `220568`. `create_user_subvolume` logs "Skipping conversion" (dir exists + non-empty) and returns without calling `ensure_owned`. Then `mkdir_p("chrome-profile")` fails because the parent is root-owned.

Fix: always call `ensure_owned` even when skipping BTRFS conversion, and handle non-BTRFS too.

# Create a BTRFS subvolume for a user's directory
    # This creates the parent user directory as a subvolume
    def create_user_subvolume(username)
      return false unless btrfs?

      user_dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{username}"

      # Check if already a subvolume
      if subvolume?(user_dir)
        Rails.logger.debug("User directory is already a BTRFS subvolume: #{user_dir}")
        ensure_owned(user_dir)
        return true
      end

      if Dir.exist?(user_dir) && !Dir.empty?(user_dir)
        # Directory already has content — skip conversion to avoid root-ownership issues
        Rails.logger.info("Skipping BTRFS subvolume conversion for existing directory: #{user_dir}")
        return false
      end

      create_subvolume(user_dir)
    end

    # Create a BTRFS subvolume for a user's data subdirectory
    def create_user_data_subvolume(username, data_path)
      return false unless btrfs?

      # Ensure parent user directory exists as subvolume
      create_user_subvolume(username)

      data_dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{username}/data/#{data_path}".chomp("/")

      # Check if already a subvolume
      if subvolume?(data_dir)
        Rails.logger.debug("Data directory is already a BTRFS subvolume: #{data_dir}")
        ensure_owned(data_dir)
        return true
      end

      if Dir.exist?(data_dir) && !Dir.empty?(data_dir)
        # Directory already has content — skip conversion to avoid root-ownership issues
        Rails.logger.info("Skipping BTRFS subvolume conversion for existing directory: #{data_dir}")
        return false
      end

      create_subvolume(data_dir)
    end
input.new_string:
# Ensure a user's base directory exists and is owned by the current process.
    # On BTRFS, creates a subvolume; on regular filesystems, creates a plain directory.
    # Always safe to call even if the directory already exists.
    def create_user_subvolume(username)
      user_dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{username}"

      if btrfs?
        if subvolume?(user_dir)
          Rails.logger.debug("User directory is already a BTRFS subvolume: #{user_dir}")
          ensure_owned(user_dir)
          return true
        end

        if Dir.exist?(user_dir)
          # Exists but not a subvolume (e.g. created by root during install) —
          # skip conversion but still fix ownership so Rails can write into it.
          Rails.logger.info("Fixing ownership of existing user directory: #{user_dir}")
          ensure_owned(user_dir)
          return false
        end

        create_subvolume(user_dir)
      else
        FileUtils.mkdir_p(user_dir) unless Dir.exist?(user_dir)
        ensure_owned(user_dir)
        false
      end
    end

    # Create a BTRFS subvolume for a user's data subdirectory
    def create_user_data_subvolume(username, data_path)
      # Ensure parent user directory exists and is owned
      create_user_subvolume(username)

      data_dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{username}/data/#{data_path}".chomp("/")

      if btrfs?
        if subvolume?(data_dir)
          Rails.logger.debug("Data directory is already a BTRFS subvolume: #{data_dir}")
          ensure_owned(data_dir)
          return true
        end

        if Dir.exist?(data_dir)
          Rails.logger.info("Fixing ownership of existing data directory: #{data_dir}")
          ensure_owned(data_dir)
          return false
        end

        create_subvolume(data_dir)
      else
        FileUtils.mkdir_p(data_dir) unless Dir.exist?(data_dir)
        ensure_owned(data_dir)
        false
      end
    end

toolUseResult.originalFile:
class BtrfsHelper
  class Error < StandardError; end

  DATA_DIR = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR", "/data")

  class << self
    # Check if a path is on a BTRFS filesystem
    def btrfs?(path = DATA_DIR)
      return @is_btrfs if defined?(@is_btrfs)

      result = system("stat -f -c %T #{path} 2>/dev/null | grep -q '^btrfs$'")
      @is_btrfs = result == true
    rescue StandardError => e
      Rails.logger.warn("BTRFS detection failed: #{e.message}")
      @is_btrfs = false
    end

    # Create a BTRFS subvolume for a user's directory
    # This creates the parent user directory as a subvolume
    def create_user_subvolume(username)
      return false unless btrfs?

      user_dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{username}"

      # Check if already a subvolume
      if subvolume?(user_dir)
        Rails.logger.debug("User directory is already a BTRFS subvolume: #{user_dir}")
        ensure_owned(user_dir)
        return true
      end

      if Dir.exist?(user_dir) && !Dir.empty?(user_dir)
        # Directory already has content — skip conversion to avoid root-ownership issues
        Rails.logger.info("Skipping BTRFS subvolume conversion for existing directory: #{user_dir}")
        return false
      end

      create_subvolume(user_dir)
    end

    # Create a BTRFS subvolume for a user's data subdirectory
    def create_user_data_subvolume(username, data_path)
      return false unless btrfs?

      # Ensure parent user directory exists as subvolume
      create_user_subvolume(username)

      data_dir = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{username}/data/#{data_path}".chomp("/")

      # Check if already a subvolume
      if subvolume?(data_dir)
        Rails.logger.debug("Data directory is already a BTRFS subvolume: #{data_dir}")
        ensure_owned(data_dir)
        return true
      end

      if Dir.exist?(data_dir) && !Dir.empty?(data_dir)
        # Directory already has content — skip conversion to avoid root-ownership issues
        Rails.logger.info("Skipping BTRFS subvolume conversion for existing directory: #{data_dir}")
        return false
      end

      create_subvolume(data_dir)
    end

    private

    # Check if a path is a BTRFS subvolume
    def subvolume?(path)
      return false unless Dir.exist?(path)

      result = system("/usr/bin/sudo /usr/bin/btrfs subvolume show #{path} >/dev/null 2>&1")
      result == true
    rescue StandardError => e
      Rails.logger.warn("BTRFS subvolume check failed for #{path}: #{e.message}")
      false
    end

    # Create a new BTRFS subvolume
    def create_subvolume(path)
      # Ensure parent directory exists
      parent = File.dirname(path)
      FileUtils.mkdir_p(parent) unless Dir.exist?(parent)

      # Create subvolume using sudo with full path
      output, status = run_sudo_command("/usr/bin/btrfs subvolume create #{path}")

      unless status.success?
        raise Error, "Failed to create BTRFS subvolume #{path}: #{output}"
      end

      ensure_owned(path)

      Rails.logger.info("Created BTRFS subvolume: #{path}")
      true
    rescue StandardError => e
      Rails.logger.error("Failed to create BTRFS subvolume #{path}: #{e.message}")
      # Fall back to regular directory
      FileUtils.mkdir_p(path) unless Dir.exist?(path)
      false
    end

    # Ensure the path is owned by the current process user
    def ensure_owned(path)
      return if File.stat(path).uid == Process.uid

      run_sudo_command("/usr/bin/chown #{Process.uid}:#{Process.gid} #{path}")
    end

    # Run a command with sudo
    def run_sudo_command(command)
      full_command = "/usr/bin/sudo -n #{command}"
      output = `#{full_command} 2>&1`
      [ output, $? ]
    rescue StandardError => e
      [ e.message, nil ]
    end
  end
end

content.text:
Done. Here's what was wrong and what changed:

**Root cause:** `/sandcastle/data/users/thies/` was owned by `root:root` because the Tailscale sidecar (which runs as root) created it first. `create_user_subvolume` hit the "skip conversion" branch (dir exists + non-empty) and returned without fixing ownership. Then `FileUtils.mkdir_p("chrome-profile")` failed because Rails (uid 220568) had no write access.

**Two-part fix applied:**

1. **Live fix:** `sudo chown 220568:220568 /sandcastle/data/users/thies` on the host + patched file copied into both running containers.

2. **Code fix** in `btrfs_helper.rb`:
   - `create_user_subvolume` now always calls `ensure_owned` — whether the dir is an existing plain dir (was root-owned), an existing subvolume, or freshly created
   - Non-BTRFS path also creates + chowns the directory instead of being a no-op
   - Same fix in `create_user_data_subvolume`

file.content:
#!/bin/bash
set -e

USERNAME="${SANDCASTLE_USER:-sandbox}"
SSH_KEY="${SANDCASTLE_SSH_KEY:-}"

# Create user if not exists
if ! id "$USERNAME" &>/dev/null; then
    useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G sudo,docker "$USERNAME"
    echo "$USERNAME ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" >> /etc/sudoers.d/sandcastle
    chmod 0440 /etc/sudoers.d/sandcastle
fi

# Set up SSH authorized keys (append if not already present, preserving
# any WeTTY keys that may have been injected for other sandboxes sharing
# this user's home directory via bind mount).
if [ -n "$SSH_KEY" ]; then
    SSH_DIR="/home/$USERNAME/.ssh"
    mkdir -p "$SSH_DIR"
    if [ -f "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" ]; then
        grep -qF "$SSH_KEY" "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys" || echo "$SSH_KEY" >> "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    else
        echo "$SSH_KEY" > "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    fi
    chmod 700 "$SSH_DIR"
    chmod 600 "$SSH_DIR/authorized_keys"
    chown -R "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "$SSH_DIR"
fi

# Ensure home directory ownership and permissions.
# The host may create bind-mounted home dirs with 777 so Sysbox-mapped root
# can write. Tighten to 755 here so sshd StrictModes is satisfied.
chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "/home/$USERNAME"
chmod 755 "/home/$USERNAME"

# Ensure workspace is accessible
chown "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" /workspace 2>/dev/null || true

# Seed mise + Claude Code into user's ~/.local/bin on first boot
USER_LOCAL_BIN="/home/$USERNAME/.local/bin"
mkdir -p "$USER_LOCAL_BIN"
for tool in mise claude; do
    if [ ! -f "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool" ] && [ -f "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" ]; then
        cp "/opt/sandcastle/bin/$tool" "$USER_LOCAL_BIN/$tool"
    fi
done
chown -R "$USERNAME:$USERNAME" "/home/$USERNAME/.local"

# Configure git identity system-wide if provided
if [ -n "$USER_FULLNAME" ] || [ -n "$USER_EMAIL" ]; then
    {
        echo "[user]"
        [ -n "$USER_FULLNAME" ] && echo "    name = $USER_FULLNAME"
        [ -n "$USER_EMAIL" ] && echo "    email = $USER_EMAIL"
    } >> /etc/gitconfig
fi

# Generate SSH host keys if missing
ssh-keygen -A

# Start Docker daemon in background (requires Sysbox runtime for isolated /var/lib/docker)
# Don't wait for it to be ready - users can check with `docker info` after SSH login
if command -v dockerd &>/dev/null && [ -e /dev/fuse ]; then
    # Match inner Docker bridge MTU to container's eth0 to avoid packet fragmentation
    ETH0_MTU=$(ip link show eth0 2>/dev/null | grep -oP 'mtu \K[0-9]+' || echo 1500)
    dockerd --storage-driver=overlay2 --mtu="$ETH0_MTU" &>/var/log/dockerd.log &
else
    echo "Note: Docker-in-Docker not available (requires sysbox-runc runtime)" >&2
fi

# Start virtual X server and VNC server for browser access
if command -v Xvfb &>/dev/null && command -v x11vnc &>/dev/null; then
    # Start Xvfb on display :99 with 1920x1080 resolution and 24-bit color depth
    Xvfb :99 -screen 0 1920x1080x24 &>/var/log/xvfb.log &
    # Wait a moment for Xvfb to initialize
    sleep 1
    # Start x11vnc server: -shared allows multiple connections, -forever keeps running,
    # -nopw allows connections without password (secured via Docker network isolation)
    # -wsport 5901 enables x11vnc's native WebSocket listener (avoids needing websockify
    # as a translation layer, which conflicts with x11vnc 0.9.17's WS auto-detection)
    DISPLAY=:99 x11vnc -shared -forever -nopw -rfbport 5900 -wsport 5901 &>/var/log/x11vnc.log &
fi

# Start SSH daemon in foreground
exec /usr/sbin/sshd -D -e

FROM ubuntu:25.10

ARG SANDCASTLE_VERSION=dev

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# System tools
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    openssh-server sudo curl git tmux vim neovim \
    build-essential \
    jq ripgrep fd-find htop wget unzip ca-certificates net-tools iputils-ping \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# GUI tools: Xvfb (virtual X server), x11vnc, and Google Chrome
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    xvfb x11vnc xfonts-base xfonts-100dpi xfonts-75dpi \
    fonts-liberation libappindicator3-1 libasound2t64 libatk-bridge2.0-0t64 \
    libatk1.0-0t64 libcups2t64 libdbus-1-3 libgdk-pixbuf-2.0-0 libnspr4 libnss3 \
    libx11-xcb1 libxcomposite1 libxdamage1 libxrandr2 xdg-utils \
    && wget -q https://dl.google.com/linux/direct/google-chrome-stable_current_amd64.deb \
    && apt-get install -y ./google-chrome-stable_current_amd64.deb \
    && rm google-chrome-stable_current_amd64.deb \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Prefer IPv4 to avoid slow/broken IPv6 connections
RUN sed -i 's/#precedence ::ffff:0:0\/96  100/precedence ::ffff:0:0\/96  100/' /etc/gai.conf

# Docker CLI + daemon (Sysbox makes this safe)
RUN curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh

# Pin runc to 1.1.x — runc 1.2+ added a procfs safety check that fails
# inside sysbox containers (sysbox-fs mounts /proc/sys as separate FUSE)
RUN RUNC_VERSION="v1.1.15" \
    && ARCH=$(dpkg --print-architecture) \
    && curl -fsSL "https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/releases/download/${RUNC_VERSION}/runc.${ARCH}" \
       -o /usr/bin/runc \
    && chmod +x /usr/bin/runc

# Docker Compose plugin
RUN mkdir -p /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins \
    && curl -fsSL "https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/latest/download/docker-compose-linux-$(uname -m)" \
       -o /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose \
    && chmod +x /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose

# Stage mise + Claude Code in /opt/sandcastle/bin (seeded to ~/.local/bin on first boot)
RUN mkdir -p /opt/sandcastle/bin \
    && curl https://mise.run | REDACTED sh \
    && curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash \
    && cp -L /root/.local/bin/claude /opt/sandcastle/bin/claude \
    && rm -rf /root/.local/share/claude /root/.local/bin/claude

# Entire CLI — git observability for AI agent sessions (https://entire.io)
RUN ARCH="$(dpkg --print-architecture)" \
    && curl -fsSL "https://github.com/entireio/cli/releases/latest/download/entire_linux_${ARCH}.tar.gz" \
       | tar -xz -C /usr/local/bin entire \
    && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/entire

# Shell config: PATH, mise activation, aliases
RUN echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' >> /etc/bash.bashrc \
    && echo 'eval "$(mise activate bash)"' >> /etc/bash.bashrc \
    && echo "alias yolo='claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'" >> /etc/bash.bashrc

# Bake version into the image
RUN echo "${SANDCASTLE_VERSION}" > /etc/sandcastle-version

# SSH configuration (key-only auth) — use drop-in config for Ubuntu 25.10 compatibility
RUN mkdir -p /var/run/sshd /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d \
    && echo "PermitRootLogin no" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/10-sandcastle.conf \
    && echo "PubkeyAuthentication yes" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/10-sandcastle.conf \
    && echo "PasswordAuthentication no" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/10-sandcastle.conf

COPY entrypoint.sh /entrypoint.sh
RUN chmod +x /entrypoint.sh

COPY tmux.conf /etc/tmux.conf
COPY gitconfig /etc/gitconfig

LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/thieso2/sandcastle"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="Sandcastle sandbox image — Ubuntu 25.10 with Docker-in-Docker, SSH, and dev tools"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

WORKDIR /workspace
EXPOSE 22 5900

ENTRYPOINT ["/entrypoint.sh"]

The Dockerfile has `x11vnc` in the package list and `EXPOSE 22 5900`. But the running container doesn't have x11vnc installed. This means the running sandbox container (`sc-vnc-thies-slow-tiger`) was created from an older version of the image that didn't have x11vnc.

content.thinking:
The `sc-vnc-thies-slow-tiger` container is running `gotget/novnc:latest` - it's the noVNC container, not the sandbox container! That's the noVNC sidecar that we create for browser-based VNC access.

Let me re-read the VNC manager to understand the architecture:
- The sandbox container runs Xvfb + x11vnc (on port 5900 for RFB, 5901 for WebSocket)
- A separate noVNC container (`sc-novnc-{sandbox}`) serves the browser UI
- Traefik routes /vnc/{id}/... traffic to the noVNC container


- The process list the user showed was from inside the actual sandbox container, but I need to find which Docker container that corresponds to since the naming is a bit confusing.

  docker ps --format "{{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Command}}" | grep sshd || true
  docker ps --format "{{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}" | grep "sandbox" || true
  docker ps --format "{{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}"
' 2>&1

input.command:
ssh sandcastle@sandman '
  echo "=== x11vnc in sandbox ==="
  docker exec thies-slow-tiger which x11vnc 2>/dev/null || echo "NOT FOUND"
  
  echo "=== image creation date ==="
  docker inspect thies-slow-tiger --format "{{.Config.Image}} created {{.Created}}"
  
  echo "=== x11vnc log ==="
  docker exec thies-slow-tiger cat /var/log/x11vnc.log 2>/dev/null || echo "no log"
  
  echo "=== xvfb log ==="
  docker exec thies-slow-tiger cat /var/log/xvfb.log 2>/dev/null | head -10 || echo "no log"
  
  echo "=== entrypoint check ==="
  docker exec thies-slow-tiger grep -n "x11vnc\|Xvfb" /entrypoint.sh 2>/dev/null || echo "not in entrypoint"
' 2>&1

content.thinking:
The log is very clear:

```
18/02/2026 05:16:33 *** unrecognized option(s) ***
18/02/2026 05:16:33 	[1]  -wsport
18/02/2026 05:16:33 	[2]  5901
```

x11vnc 0.9.17 doesn't support `-wsport`! The `-wsport` option was added in a later version. The version in the Ubuntu 25.10 package is 0.9.17 from 2025-04-11, and it doesn't support `-wsport`.


So I need to go back to using websockify as the WebSocket proxy instead of relying on native x11vnc WebSocket support. The vnc_manager.rb was recently refactored to split into separate components, so I'll need to adjust the approach accordingly.

Looking at the current architecture, the issue is clear: Traefik is routing WebSocket traffic directly to x11vnc's port 5901, but since x11vnc 0.9.17 doesn't actually support WebSocket natively, that connection fails. I need to insert websockify back into the chain to bridge between WebSocket clients and the VNC protocol on port 5901. needs to happen:**
- Option A: Use websockify as the WebSocket bridge (websockify listens on port 6081 and proxies to x11vnc port 5900 RFB)
- Option B: Use the full noVNC container (which includes websockify) to serve both static files AND proxy WS
- Option C: Find a way to make x11vnc do native WebSocket (needs newer version or build from source)

Looking at the noVNC image `gotget/novnc:latest` - what does it provide? The recent VNC commit said "noVNC container runs `python3 -m http.server`" to serve static files only. So the noVNC container is now just serving HTML/JS.

The original architecture (before the recent refactor) had noVNC doing everything via websockify. Let me trace through the git history to understand what changed. The commits show a progression of fixes around WebSocket paths and routing through Traefik, culminating in a refactor that split the static file serving from WebSocket proxying and assumed x11vnc could handle native WebSocket connections directly.

Since x11vnc doesn't support native WebSocket, I need to revert to using websockify. The cleanest approach is Option 1 - run websockify inside the sandbox container itself, listening on port 5901 and proxying to x11vnc on 5900. This keeps the architecture simple and doesn't require spinning up additional containers.

Let me verify whether websockify is already available in the sandbox image.

content.thinking:
So x11vnc 0.9.17 has SSL WebSocket options (`-sslkeyfile`, `-sslcertfile`) but NOT a WebSocket port option (`-wsport`). That means it can do WebSocket but only for SSL/encrypted connections, not plain WS.

`websockify` is not installed in the sandbox container.

The approach needs to change:

**Option 1**: Add websockify to the sandbox image Dockerfile. Then start it in entrypoint.sh to listen on 5901 and proxy to 5900.

**Option 2**: Change the architecture to route WebSocket through the noVNC sidecar container (which typically comes with websockify), keeping all WS proxying outside the sandbox.
 
**Option 3**: Go back to the original architecture where the noVNC container itself handles both serving the HTML interface and proxying WebSocket connections to the sandbox's RFB port. Let me check what vnc_manager.rb is currently doing to understand the refactoring that happened.

content.content:
1→class VncManager
     2→  DATA_DIR = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR", "/data")
     3→  NOVNC_IMAGE = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_NOVNC_IMAGE", "gotget/novnc:latest")
     4→  NETWORK_NAME = "sandcastle-web"
     5→  DYNAMIC_DIR = File.join(DATA_DIR, "traefik", "dynamic")
     6→
     7→  class Error < StandardError; end
     8→
     9→  # Opens a web-based VNC session for the given sandbox.
    10→  # Returns the URL path to the noVNC session.
    11→  def open(sandbox:)
    12→    raise Error, "Sandbox is not running" unless sandbox.status == "running"
    13→    raise Error, "Sandbox has no container" if sandbox.container_id.blank?
    14→
    15→    user = sandbox.user
    16→    container_name = vnc_container_name(sandbox)
    17→
    18→    # Idempotent: if noVNC container already running, return URL
    19→    if container_running?(container_name)
    20→      return vnc_url(sandbox)
    21→    end
    22→
    23→    pull_image
    24→    ensure_network
    25→    connect_sandbox_to_network(sandbox)
    26→
    27→    # Write Traefik config early so it has time to detect the new route
    28→    write_traefik_config(sandbox)
    29→
    30→    create_novnc_container(sandbox: sandbox, user: user)
    31→
    32→    # Give Traefik time to detect the new route configuration
    33→    sleep 2
    34→
    35→    vnc_url(sandbox)
    36→  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
    37→    raise Error, "Failed to open browser: #{e.message}"
    38→  rescue SystemCallError => e
    39→    raise Error, "Failed to open browser: #{e.message}"
    40→  end
    41→
    42→  # Closes the web VNC session for the given sandbox.
    43→  def close(sandbox:)
    44→    container_name = vnc_container_name(sandbox)
    45→
    46→    # Stop and remove noVNC container
    47→    begin
    48→      container = Docker::Container.get(container_name)
    49→      container.stop(t: 3) rescue nil
    50→      container.delete(force: true)
    51→    rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
    52→      # Already gone
    53→    end
    54→
    55→    # Delete Traefik config
    56→    delete_traefik_config(sandbox)
    57→  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
    58→    Rails.logger.error("VncManager: close failed for #{sandbox.full_name}: #{e.message}")
    59→  end
    60→
    61→  # Returns true if the noVNC container is running for this sandbox.
    62→  def active?(sandbox:)
    63→    container_running?(vnc_container_name(sandbox))
    64→  end
    65→
    66→  # Removes orphaned noVNC containers whose sandbox no longer exists or is not running.
    67→  def cleanup_orphaned
    68→    Docker::Container.all(all: true).each do |container|
    69→      name = container.info.dig("Names")&.first&.delete_prefix("/")
    70→      next unless name&.start_with?("sc-vnc-")
    71→
    72→      labels = container.info["Labels"] || {}
    73→      sandbox_id = labels["sandcastle.sandbox_id"]&.to_i
    74→
    75→      sandbox = sandbox_id ? Sandbox.find_by(id: sandbox_id) : nil
    76→      should_remove = sandbox.nil? || sandbox.status != "running"
    77→
    78→      if should_remove
    79→        container.stop(t: 3) rescue nil
    80→        container.delete(force: true)
    81→        Rails.logger.info("VncManager: removed orphaned noVNC container #{name}")
    82→
    83→        # Clean up Traefik config if we have a sandbox_id
    84→        if sandbox_id
    85→          config_path = File.join(DYNAMIC_DIR, "vnc-#{sandbox_id}.yml")
    86→          File.delete(config_path) if File.exist?(config_path)
    87→        end
    88→      end
    89→    end
    90→  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
    91→    Rails.logger.error("VncManager: orphan cleanup failed: #{e.message}")
    92→  end
    93→
    94→  private
    95→
    96→  def vnc_container_name(sandbox)
    97→    "sc-vnc-#{sandbox.full_name}"
    98→  end
    99→
   100→  def vnc_url(sandbox)
   101→    id = sandbox.id
   102→    # noVNC builds the WebSocket URL as wss://host/<path setting>.
   103→    # Default path is "websockify". Without ?path=..., noVNC would try
   104→    # wss://host/websockify which Traefik has no route for.
   105→    # Pass the full routed path so Traefik's stripPrefix reduces it to
   106→    # /websockify before websockify receives the connection.
   107→    "/vnc/#{id}/novnc/vnc.html?path=vnc/#{id}/novnc/websockify"
   108→  end
   109→
   110→  def container_running?(name)
   111→    container = Docker::Container.get(name)
   112→    container.json.dig("State", "Running") == true
   113→  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
   114→    false
   115→  end
   116→
   117→  def pull_image
   118→    Docker::Image.get(NOVNC_IMAGE)
   119→  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
   120→    Docker::Image.create("fromImage" => NOVNC_IMAGE)
   121→  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError
   122→    raise Error, "Failed to pull #{NOVNC_IMAGE} — check network connectivity"
   123→  end
   124→
   125→  def ensure_network
   126→    Docker::Network.get(NETWORK_NAME)
   127→  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
   128→    Docker::Network.create(NETWORK_NAME, "Driver" => "bridge")
   129→  end
   130→
   131→  def connect_sandbox_to_network(sandbox)
   132→    return unless sandbox.container_id.present?
   133→
   134→    network = Docker::Network.get(NETWORK_NAME)
   135→    container = Docker::Container.get(sandbox.container_id)
   136→
   137→    networks = container.json.dig("NetworkSettings", "Networks") || {}
   138→    return if networks.key?(NETWORK_NAME)
   139→
   140→    network.connect(sandbox.container_id)
   141→  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
   142→    # Container no longer exists - sync job will fix the DB state
   143→    raise Error, "Sandbox container not found. Please refresh and try again."
   144→  end
   145→
   146→  def create_novnc_container(sandbox:, user:)
   147→    container_name = vnc_container_name(sandbox)
   148→
   149→    # Remove any existing container with this name
   150→    begin
   151→      old = Docker::Container.get(container_name)
   152→      old.stop(t: 3) rescue nil

class VncManager
  DATA_DIR = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_DATA_DIR", "/data")
  NOVNC_IMAGE = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_NOVNC_IMAGE", "gotget/novnc:latest")
  NETWORK_NAME = "sandcastle-web"
  DYNAMIC_DIR = File.join(DATA_DIR, "traefik", "dynamic")

  class Error < StandardError; end

  # Opens a web-based VNC session for the given sandbox.
  # Returns the URL path to the noVNC session.
  def open(sandbox:)
    raise Error, "Sandbox is not running" unless sandbox.status == "running"
    raise Error, "Sandbox has no container" if sandbox.container_id.blank?

    user = sandbox.user
    container_name = vnc_container_name(sandbox)

    # Idempotent: if noVNC container already running, return URL
    if container_running?(container_name)
      return vnc_url(sandbox)
    end

    pull_image
    ensure_network
    connect_sandbox_to_network(sandbox)

    # Write Traefik config early so it has time to detect the new route
    write_traefik_config(sandbox)

    create_novnc_container(sandbox: sandbox, user: user)

    # Give Traefik time to detect the new route configuration
    sleep 2

    vnc_url(sandbox)
  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
    raise Error, "Failed to open browser: #{e.message}"
  rescue SystemCallError => e
    raise Error, "Failed to open browser: #{e.message}"
  end

  # Closes the web VNC session for the given sandbox.
  def close(sandbox:)
    container_name = vnc_container_name(sandbox)

    # Stop and remove noVNC container
    begin
      container = Docker::Container.get(container_name)
      container.stop(t: 3) rescue nil
      container.delete(force: true)
    rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
      # Already gone
    end

    # Delete Traefik config
    delete_traefik_config(sandbox)
  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
    Rails.logger.error("VncManager: close failed for #{sandbox.full_name}: #{e.message}")
  end

  # Returns true if the noVNC container is running for this sandbox.
  def active?(sandbox:)
    container_running?(vnc_container_name(sandbox))
  end

  # Removes orphaned noVNC containers whose sandbox no longer exists or is not running.
  def cleanup_orphaned
    Docker::Container.all(all: true).each do |container|
      name = container.info.dig("Names")&.first&.delete_prefix("/")
      next unless name&.start_with?("sc-vnc-")

      labels = container.info["Labels"] || {}
      sandbox_id = labels["sandcastle.sandbox_id"]&.to_i

      sandbox = sandbox_id ? Sandbox.find_by(id: sandbox_id) : nil
      should_remove = sandbox.nil? || sandbox.status != "running"

      if should_remove
        container.stop(t: 3) rescue nil
        container.delete(force: true)
        Rails.logger.info("VncManager: removed orphaned noVNC container #{name}")

        # Clean up Traefik config if we have a sandbox_id
        if sandbox_id
          config_path = File.join(DYNAMIC_DIR, "vnc-#{sandbox_id}.yml")
          File.delete(config_path) if File.exist?(config_path)
        end
      end
    end
  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
    Rails.logger.error("VncManager: orphan cleanup failed: #{e.message}")
  end

  private

  def vnc_container_name(sandbox)
    "sc-vnc-#{sandbox.full_name}"
  end

  def vnc_url(sandbox)
    id = sandbox.id
    # noVNC builds the WebSocket URL as wss://host/<path setting>.
    # Default path is "websockify". Without ?path=..., noVNC would try
    # wss://host/websockify which Traefik has no route for.
    # Pass the full routed path so Traefik's stripPrefix reduces it to
    # /websockify before websockify receives the connection.
    "/vnc/#{id}/novnc/vnc.html?path=vnc/#{id}/novnc/websockify"
  end

  def container_running?(name)
    container = Docker::Container.get(name)
    container.json.dig("State", "Running") == true
  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
    false
  end

  def pull_image
    Docker::Image.get(NOVNC_IMAGE)
  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
    Docker::Image.create("fromImage" => NOVNC_IMAGE)
  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError
    raise Error, "Failed to pull #{NOVNC_IMAGE} — check network connectivity"
  end

  def ensure_network
    Docker::Network.get(NETWORK_NAME)
  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
    Docker::Network.create(NETWORK_NAME, "Driver" => "bridge")
  end

  def connect_sandbox_to_network(sandbox)
    return unless sandbox.container_id.present?

    network = Docker::Network.get(NETWORK_NAME)
    container = Docker::Container.get(sandbox.container_id)

    networks = container.json.dig("NetworkSettings", "Networks") || {}
    return if networks.key?(NETWORK_NAME)

    network.connect(sandbox.container_id)
  rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
    # Container no longer exists - sync job will fix the DB state
    raise Error, "Sandbox container not found. Please refresh and try again."
  end

  def create_novnc_container(sandbox:, user:)
    container_name = vnc_container_name(sandbox)

    # Remove any existing container with this name
    begin
      old = Docker::Container.get(container_name)
      old.stop(t: 3) rescue nil
      old.delete(force: true)
    rescue Docker::Error::NotFoundError
      # No existing container
    end

    host_config = {
      "NetworkMode" => NETWORK_NAME,
      "RestartPolicy" => { "Name" => "no" },
      "Memory" => 64 * 1024 * 1024, # 64MB — just serving static HTML
      "NanoCpus" => 100_000_000 # 0.1 CPU
    }

    # Run the noVNC image as a plain HTTP file server for noVNC static assets.
    # WebSocket VNC connections are routed by Traefik directly to x11vnc's
    # native WebSocket port (5901) in the sandbox — no websockify needed.
    container = Docker::Container.create(
      "name" => container_name,
      "Image" => NOVNC_IMAGE,
      "Entrypoint" => [ "python3", "-m", "http.server", "--directory", "/usr/src/app/noVNC", "6080" ],
      "HostConfig" => host_config,
      "Labels" => {
        "sandcastle.sandbox_id" => sandbox.id.to_s,
        "sandcastle.role" => "novnc"
      }
    )

    container.start
    container
  end

  def write_traefik_config(sandbox)
    FileUtils.mkdir_p(DYNAMIC_DIR)

    host = ENV.fetch("SANDCASTLE_HOST", "localhost")
    id = sandbox.id
    container_name = vnc_container_name(sandbox)
    sandbox_name   = sandbox.full_name

    base_rule = if ENV["SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE"] == "selfsigned"
      "HostRegexp(`.+`) && PathPrefix(`/vnc/#{id}/novnc`)"
    else
      "Host(`#{host}`) && PathPrefix(`/vnc/#{id}/novnc`)"
    end

    # Two routers on the same path prefix:
    # - HTTP router  → noVNC static files container (port 6080)
    # - WebSocket router → x11vnc native WebSocket in the sandbox (port 5901)
    # Splitting avoids routing WebSocket traffic through the old gotget/novnc
    # websockify, which sends raw WS frames that x11vnc 0.9.17 misidentifies.
    config = {
      "http" => {
        "routers" => {
          "vnc-html-#{id}" => {
            "rule" => "#{base_rule} && !HeaderRegexp(`Upgrade`, `websocket`)",
            "service" => "vnc-html-#{id}",
            "entryPoints" => [ "websecure" ],
            "tls" => tls_config,
            "middlewares" => [ "vnc-auth-#{id}", "vnc-stripprefix-#{id}" ],
            "priority" => 101
          },
          "vnc-ws-#{id}" => {
            "rule" => "#{base_rule} && HeaderRegexp(`Upgrade`, `websocket`)",
            "service" => "vnc-ws-#{id}",
            "entryPoints" => [ "websecure" ],
            "tls" => tls_config,
            "middlewares" => [ "vnc-auth-#{id}", "vnc-stripprefix-#{id}" ],
            "priority" => 101
          }
        },
        "middlewares" => {
          "vnc-auth-#{id}" => {
            "forwardAuth" => {
              "address" => "http://sandcastle-web:80/vnc/auth",
              "trustForwardHeader" => true
            }
          },
          "vnc-stripprefix-#{id}" => {
            "stripPrefix" => {
              "prefixes" => [ "/vnc/#{id}/novnc" ]
            }
          }
        },
        "services" => {
          # noVNC HTML/JS/CSS static files
          "vnc-html-#{id}" => {
            "loadBalancer" => {
              "servers" => [ { "url" => "http://#{container_name}:6080" } ]
            }
          },
          # x11vnc native WebSocket (bypasses websockify translation layer)
          "vnc-ws-#{id}" => {
            "loadBalancer" => {
              "servers" => [ { "url" => "http://#{sandbox_name}:5901" } ]
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }

    File.write(File.join(DYNAMIC_DIR, "vnc-#{id}.yml"), config.to_yaml)
  end

  def tls_config
    if ENV["SANDCASTLE_TLS_MODE"] == "selfsigned"
      {}
    else
      { "certResolver" => "letsencrypt" }
    end
  end

  def delete_traefik_config(sandbox)
    path = File.join(DYNAMIC_DIR, "vnc-#{sandbox.id}.yml")
    File.delete(path) if File.exist?(path)
  end
end

content.thinking:
OK, now I understand the full picture. Here's the situation:

1. x11vnc 0.9.17 doesn't support `-wsport` — it crashes immediately after logging "unrecognized option(s): -wsport 5901"
2. So the sandbox has no VNC server running at all (neither RFB nor WS)
3. The current vnc_manager.rb routes WebSocket to `sandbox:5901` which doesn't exist

The cleanest fix:

**Option A (recommended):** Use the original noVNC container approach where the noVNC sidecar runs websockify (bundled in gotget/novnc image) to proxy WS → RFB to the sandbox's x11vnc port 5900.


The gotget/novnc image can be started with a flag pointing to the sandbox's VNC port, which internally launches websockify to handle the proxying. So I need to strip the unsupported `-wsport` flag from the sandbox's x11vnc startup, keep it listening on the standard RFB port 5900, and let the noVNC sidecar handle all the WebSocket routing through its bundled websockify instance. websockify. Option A is simpler since it doesn't require rebuilding the image—I just need to remove the `-wsport` flag from the entrypoint. Looking at the git history, the gotget/novnc image is actually designed to handle websockify internally when run with the `--vnc` argument, so the recent refactor that changed the entrypoint to a plain HTTP server broke that functionality. The fix involves reverting the container creation to use the image properly with `--vnc sandbox_name:5900`, removing the `-wsport 5901` flag from entrypoint.sh since x11vnc doesn't support it, and simplifying the Traefik configuration to a single router since the noVNC container handles both HTML and WebSocket traffic. The URL structure remains the same with the websockify path parameter.

content.thinking:
The `gotget/novnc` image's default entrypoint is `launch.sh` which starts both the WebSocket proxy (websockify) AND a mini-webserver. It takes `--vnc HOST:PORT` to specify the VNC server to connect to. This is the correct way to use this image.

So the complete fix is:

1. **entrypoint.sh**: Remove `-wsport 5901` from the x11vnc command (it's not supported). Just use `-rfbport 5900`.

2. **vnc_manager.rb**: 
   - Change `create_novnc_container` to use the default entrypoint of `gotget/novnc` with `--vnc sandbox_name:5900`
   - Simplify the Traefik configuration back to a single router since noVNC's launch.sh handles both static files and WebSocket proxying on port 6080
   - Remove the split router approach that was trying to separate HTML and WebSocket traffic
   - The URL path `/vnc/{id}/novnc/vnc.html?path=vnc/{id}/novnc/websockify` already works correctly — Traefik strips the prefix and routes everything to port 6080, where noVNC serves HTML at `/vnc.html` and websockify listens at `/websockify`
   - The main fixes are removing the `-wsport 5901` flag from the entrypoint and updating the vnc_manager.rb configuration accordingly
   - I need to adjust the memory allocation to at least 128MB since websockify requires more than 64MB, and configure the noVNC container to start with `--vnc sandbox_name:5900 --listen 6080` to properly connect to the VNC server and serve on the expected port
   - For Traefik, I'll set up a single router that directs traffic from `/vnc/{id}/novnc` to the noVNC container on port 6080 with prefix stripping, and bump the CPU allocation to 0.25 to handle the additional load

content.text:
The `gotget/novnc` image's built-in `launch.sh` does exactly what we need — serves noVNC static files **and** runs websockify on the same port 6080. We just need to pass `--vnc sandbox:5900`. The split-router approach was wrong. Fix both files:

**`entrypoint.sh`** — remove the unsupported `-wsport` flag  
**`vnc_manager.rb`** — revert to single router, noVNC container handles websockify
